content.text: Contrastive learning trains an embedding model by presenting it with pairs of semantically related texts (positives) and semantically unrelated texts (negatives), and optimising the model so that positives land close together in vector space while negatives are pushed apart. It is the dominant paradigm behind every major open-source embedding model — BGE, E5, GTE, nomic-embed, and Cohere — and produces representations that generalise to semantic search, clustering, deduplication, and retrieval-augmented generation without any task-specific classifier heads.
What is Contrastive Learning

Contrastive learning is a self-supervised or weakly supervised training objective that teaches an encoder what 'similar' means for a given domain, without needing a predefined label taxonomy. Given an anchor text, the model is simultaneously attracted towards a positive example (a text that should be close in meaning) and repelled from one or more negatives (texts that should be semantically distant).

The anchor, positive, and negative are all encoded by the same shared encoder (a Siamese or twin network). The loss function measures distances or similarities between the resulting embeddings and computes a gradient that nudges the encoder weights toward a geometry where semantic similarity is reflected as cosine similarity.

Unlike classification objectives that learn 'label X means class Y', contrastive objectives learn a relative notion of similarity. This makes them highly transferable: a model fine-tuned to distinguish medical query–document pairs often transfers to legal or scientific retrieval without retraining.

The fundamental difference from supervised classification is that contrastive learning does not require a fixed set of categories. It only requires a signal about which texts are semantically related — something that can often be derived cheaply from structure in the data: title–body pairs, question–answer pairs, duplicate forum posts, or even the same sentence run through two different dropout masks (SimCSE).
Why it Matters

Before contrastive learning became dominant (roughly 2019–2021), text embedding models were built primarily on masked language modelling (BERT, RoBERTa). These models produce strong contextual representations but their pooled sentence embeddings are not well-calibrated for semantic similarity — they cluster around a narrow region of the embedding space, and cosine similarity between two sentence embeddings often fails to correlate with human judgements of semantic similarity.

Contrastive fine-tuning fixes this. By explicitly training the model to produce embeddings where similar sentences cluster together and dissimilar ones are separated, the resulting embeddings are directly usable for semantic search, clustering, and duplicate detection without further processing. The improvement is dramatic: sentence-BERT (a contrastive model) reduced the time to find similar sentences in a 10,000 sentence corpus from 65 hours (cross-encoder exhaustive) to about 5 seconds, with only a minor quality drop.
Powers all state-of-the-art open-source embedding models (BGE, E5, GTE, nomic-embed, Cohere embed).
Enables semantic search without predefined label taxonomies or domain-specific ontologies.
Transfers remarkably well across domains: a model trained on web text pairs retrieves medical documents better than BM25.
Scales to weak supervision: translation pairs, title-body pairs, and question-answer pairs from web data require no manual annotation.
Works for both symmetric tasks (clustering, deduplication) and asymmetric tasks (query-to-document retrieval).
Produces isotropic embeddings — uniformly distributed in the embedding hypersphere — which fixes the anisotropy problem of masked-LM pooled embeddings.
Benefits
No label taxonomy required: only a notion of similar/dissimilar pairs, which can often be derived from data structure.
Works with weak supervision at massive scale: CC-pairs, Reddit question-answer pairs, Wikipedia title-body pairs are all freely available.
Highly transferable: a single contrastively trained backbone performs well across dozens of different retrieval benchmarks.
Compositional: a base model can be domain-adapted with only 1,000–10,000 domain-specific pairs using contrastive fine-tuning.
Produces calibrated similarities: cosine(a, b) > 0.8 consistently signals semantic near-duplication, enabling threshold-based filtering.
Compatible with asymmetric architectures: separate query and passage encoders (or prompt prefixes) can be trained jointly.
Supports hard negative mining: the model and index interact — harder negatives are identified as training progresses, creating a self-improving curriculum.
Trade-offs

Quality is highly sensitive to negative quality: easy random negatives provide weak gradient signal; the model stops improving quickly. Hard negatives are essential for competitive performance but expensive to mine.
False negatives are a real risk: in dense in-batch negative sampling, a passage that IS relevant to a query may be accidentally treated as a negative. This corrupts the gradient and must be filtered.
Large batch sizes are critical for in-batch negative objectives: with batch size 32, each query sees only 31 negatives. With batch size 512, it sees 511. Small batches dramatically underfit.
Training instability can occur with very low temperatures in InfoNCE loss: the gradient becomes too large for hard examples near the classification boundary.

Mode collapse: without sufficient diversity in training pairs, the model can collapse to a degenerate solution where all embeddings converge to the same point. Monitored by tracking embedding variance.
Requires significant compute for mining-based hard negative strategies: you must encode the full corpus and run ANN search at each epoch.
Evaluation is harder than classification: you need retrieval benchmarks (recall@k) rather than simple accuracy metrics.
How it Works
The Embedding Space

A text encoder maps a variable-length input to a fixed-size vector in a high-dimensional space (typically 256–4096 dimensions). In a randomly initialised or masked-LM trained encoder, this space has poor geometry for semantic similarity: the embeddings occupy a narrow cone (anisotropy), and cosine similarity between unrelated sentences is surprisingly high — often 0.6–0.9.

Contrastive training reshapes this space. By minimising the distance between positive pairs and maximising the distance to negatives on a per-batch or per-example basis, the training gradually spreads embeddings across the full hypersphere (uniformity) while keeping semantically related texts close (alignment). Both alignment and uniformity are measurable properties used to diagnose contrastive training quality.
The Contrastive Objective

All contrastive losses share the same core idea: for an anchor a and positive p+, the distance d(a, p+) should be small, and for a negative n-, the distance d(a, n-) should be large. They differ in how they formalise this and how many negatives they use.
The three most important loss functions are Triplet Loss, NT-Xent (InfoNCE), and MultipleNegativesRankingLoss. Each has distinct properties:

attrs.latex: L_{\text{triplet}}(a, p^+, n^-) = \max(0,\; d(a, p^+) - d(a, n^-) + m)

content.text: where m is a margin (typically 0.2–0.5) and d is cosine distance (1 - cosine similarity). The triplet loss requires explicit (anchor, positive, negative) triplets and suffers from slow convergence because many triplets are 'easy' (the constraint is already satisfied).

where tau is a temperature hyperparameter (typically 0.05–0.1) and N is the number of negatives (typically all other passages in the batch). InfoNCE treats the problem as N+1-way classification: the model must identify the positive among N negatives. The temperature sharpens or flattens the distribution — low tau makes the model more confident but less stable.

MultipleNegativesRankingLoss (MNRL) is a cross-entropy loss over a batch of B pairs. Each anchor must identify its own positive among all B passages. It is the simplest and most effective loss in practice, used by default in sentence-transformers for fine-tuning.
Architecture and Training Flow

attrs.alt: Contrastive learning training objective: anchor/positive/negative pairs through a shared encoder, before/after embedding space, and the three main loss functions

content.text: The standard architecture for contrastive embedding training is a Siamese (twin) network: a single transformer encoder that processes each text independently. During training, the anchor, positive, and negative are each passed through the encoder separately — they share all weights. The output is mean-pooled over non-padding tokens, then L2-normalised to unit length.
Siamese Network Structure

The shared-weights design is key: if anchor and positive use separate encoders, the model cannot learn what similarity means — it just learns to match encoder 1's distribution to encoder 2's distribution. With shared weights, every update that makes the positive representation closer to the anchor also makes the anchor representation more coherent, regularising the whole embedding space.

The L2 normalisation step maps embeddings onto the unit hypersphere. This is critical because it makes the inner product equivalent to cosine similarity, enables direct comparability of similarity scores across different pairs, and allows FAISS IndexFlatIP to perform exact cosine retrieval efficiently.
Forward Pass Details

For a batch of B (query, passage) pairs, the forward pass encodes 2B texts. A batch size of 32 pairs requires encoding 64 texts per step. The similarity matrix is computed as the outer product of the query embeddings and passage embeddings — a (B, B) matrix where diagonal entries are positive pairs and off-diagonal entries are negatives. The loss is then the cross-entropy of this matrix with diagonal labels [0, 1, 2, ..., B-1].

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


def mean_pool(hidden: torch.Tensor, mask: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Mean-pool token embeddings, ignoring padding."""
    mask_f = mask.unsqueeze(-1).float()
    return (hidden * mask_f).sum(1) / mask_f.sum(1).clamp(min=1e-9)


class SiameseEncoder(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, model_name: str = "BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5"):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = AutoModel.from_pretrained(model_name)
        self.tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)

    def encode_texts(self, texts: list[str], device: str = "cpu") -> torch.Tensor:
        """Encode texts to L2-normalised embeddings."""
        inputs = self.tokenizer(
            texts,
            padding=True,
            truncation=True,
            max_length=512,
            return_tensors="pt",
        ).to(device)
        out = self.encoder(**inputs)
        pooled = mean_pool(out.last_hidden_state, inputs["attention_mask"])
        return F.normalize(pooled, dim=-1)  # (B, D) on unit sphere

    def forward(self, anchors, positives):
        """
        Compute MultipleNegativesRankingLoss for a batch.
        anchors:   list of B query strings
        positives: list of B passage strings
        Returns scalar loss.
        """
        emb_a = self.encode_texts(anchors)    # (B, D)
        emb_p = self.encode_texts(positives)  # (B, D)
        sim_matrix = emb_a @ emb_p.T          # (B, B) cosine similarities
        # Labels: row i's positive is column i
        labels = torch.arange(len(anchors), device=emb_a.device)
        return F.cross_entropy(sim_matrix * 20, labels)  # scale=20 acts as 1/tau


model = SiameseEncoder()
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-5, weight_decay=1e-2)

# Training step
queries   = ["What is aspirin?",       "How does insulin work?"]
passages  = ["Aspirin inhibits COX.",  "Insulin lowers blood glucose."]
loss = model(queries, passages)
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()
optimizer.zero_grad()
print(f"Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
Loss Functions in Depth
Triplet Loss

Triplet loss is the historically first contrastive loss used for embedding training (Facenet, 2015). It requires explicit triplets (anchor, positive, negative) and penalises configurations where the negative is not at least m further from the anchor than the positive.

The main practical problem with triplet loss is that most triplets become 'satisfied' (loss = 0) quickly into training. Mining informative triplets — where the negative is close enough to the positive that the model struggles — requires expensive hard negative mining strategies. Without this, the model stops learning early.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel


class TripletLoss(nn.Module):
    """
    Triplet margin loss with cosine distance.
    L = max(0, cosine_dist(a, p+) - cosine_dist(a, n-) + margin)
    cosine_dist = 1 - cosine_similarity
    """
    def __init__(self, margin: float = 0.3):
        super().__init__()
        self.margin = margin

    def forward(
        self,
        anchor: torch.Tensor,    # (B, D) L2-normalised
        positive: torch.Tensor,  # (B, D) L2-normalised
        negative: torch.Tensor,  # (B, D) L2-normalised
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        dist_ap = 1.0 - F.cosine_similarity(anchor, positive, dim=-1)  # (B,)
        dist_an = 1.0 - F.cosine_similarity(anchor, negative, dim=-1)  # (B,)
        losses = F.relu(dist_ap - dist_an + self.margin)               # (B,)
        # Optional: only count non-zero losses (semi-hard mining filter)
        num_active = (losses > 0).sum().clamp(min=1)
        return losses.sum() / num_active


# Manual training loop with triplet loss
def mean_pool(hidden, mask):
    m = mask.unsqueeze(-1).float()
    return (hidden * m).sum(1) / m.sum(1).clamp(min=1e-9)


def encode(model, tokenizer, texts, device="cpu"):
    inp = tokenizer(texts, padding=True, truncation=True,
                    max_length=512, return_tensors="pt").to(device)
    with torch.set_grad_enabled(model.training):
        out = model(**inp)
    return F.normalize(mean_pool(out.last_hidden_state, inp["attention_mask"]), dim=-1)


tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
model     = AutoModel.from_pretrained("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
criterion = TripletLoss(margin=0.3)
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-5)

triplets = [
    ("What is aspirin?",
     "Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1 and COX-2.",
     "Ibuprofen also inhibits COX enzymes and reduces fever."),  # hard negative
    ("How is warfarin dosed?",
     "Warfarin dose is adjusted based on INR target 2–3.",
     "Warfarin inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors."),  # plausible but different
]

for epoch in range(5):
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0.0
    for anc_txt, pos_txt, neg_txt in triplets:
        a = encode(model, tokenizer, [anc_txt])
        p = encode(model, tokenizer, [pos_txt])
        n = encode(model, tokenizer, [neg_txt])
        loss = criterion(a, p, n)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}: avg loss = {total_loss / len(triplets):.4f}")

The margin hyperparameter controls how strictly the model must separate positives from negatives. A margin too small lets many triplets pass without contribution; too large forces unrealistic separations and destabilises training. Values in the range 0.2–0.5 are typical for cosine distance.
NT-Xent and InfoNCE Loss

NT-Xent (Normalised Temperature-scaled Cross Entropy), also known as InfoNCE, reframes contrastive learning as an N-way classification problem. For each anchor in the batch, the model must identify the single positive among N other passages (the in-batch negatives). The temperature tau controls the sharpness of the distribution.

With low temperature (tau ≈ 0.05–0.07), the model is very confident: the loss concentrates on the hardest negative in the batch, producing a strong gradient. With high temperature (tau ≈ 0.5–1.0), the distribution is flatter, and the loss is averaged more evenly across all negatives — producing weaker but more stable gradients.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


def nt_xent_loss(
    emb_a: torch.Tensor,       # (B, D) L2-normalised
    emb_b: torch.Tensor,       # (B, D) L2-normalised
    temperature: float = 0.07,
    symmetric: bool = True,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    NT-Xent (InfoNCE) loss for a batch of B (a_i, b_i) positive pairs.

    Each a_i's positive is its corresponding b_i.
    All other b_j (j ≠ i) are treated as negatives.

    If symmetric=True, also computes b→a direction for a more stable gradient.
    """
    B = emb_a.size(0)
    # Full cross-batch similarity matrix, scaled by temperature
    sim = (emb_a @ emb_b.T) / temperature   # (B, B)
    labels = torch.arange(B, device=emb_a.device)
    loss_ab = F.cross_entropy(sim,   labels)
    if not symmetric:
        return loss_ab
    loss_ba = F.cross_entropy(sim.T, labels)
    return (loss_ab + loss_ba) / 2


def demo_temperature_effect():
    """Visualise how temperature changes the probability distribution."""
    import numpy as np
    # Assume query has cosine similarity 0.90 with its positive
    # and varying similarities with 3 negatives
    sims = torch.tensor([0.90, 0.60, 0.30, 0.10])
    print(f"{'tau':>6}  {'P(pos)':>8}  {'P(neg1)':>8}  {'P(neg2)':>8}  {'P(neg3)':>8}")
    for tau in [0.05, 0.07, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50]:
        logits = sims / tau
        probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=0)
        print(f"{tau:>6.2f}  {probs[0].item():>8.4f}  {probs[1].item():>8.4f}  "
              f"{probs[2].item():>8.4f}  {probs[3].item():>8.4f}")
    # tau=0.05:  P(pos)=1.0000 — too peaked, unstable gradient on hard negatives
    # tau=0.07:  P(pos)=0.9998 — good balance, hard negatives still contribute
    # tau=0.20:  P(pos)=0.8225 — softer, more learning signal from easy negatives
    # tau=0.50:  P(pos)=0.4782 — too flat, weak gradient

demo_temperature_effect()

# Practical usage with an encoder
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch.nn.functional as F

def encode_batch(model, tokenizer, texts):
    inp = tokenizer(texts, padding=True, truncation=True,
                    max_length=512, return_tensors="pt")
    out = model(**inp)
    # Mean pool
    mask = inp["attention_mask"].unsqueeze(-1).float()
    pooled = (out.last_hidden_state * mask).sum(1) / mask.sum(1).clamp(min=1e-9)
    return F.normalize(pooled, dim=-1)

model_name = "BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5"
tokenizer  = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model      = AutoModel.from_pretrained(model_name)
optimizer  = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-5)

batch_queries   = ["How does aspirin work?",  "What is diabetes?"]
batch_passages  = ["Aspirin inhibits COX-1.", "Diabetes is insulin dysregulation."]

model.train()
emb_q = encode_batch(model, tokenizer, batch_queries)
emb_p = encode_batch(model, tokenizer, batch_passages)
loss  = nt_xent_loss(emb_q, emb_p, temperature=0.07, symmetric=True)
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()
optimizer.zero_grad()
print(f"NT-Xent loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
MultipleNegativesRankingLoss

MultipleNegativesRankingLoss (MNRL) is a simplification of InfoNCE that omits the temperature parameter. It computes the dot product similarity matrix between all queries and all passages in the batch, then applies cross-entropy with diagonal labels. The pre-multiplied scale (often 20 in sentence-transformers) acts as a fixed inverse temperature.

MNRL is the recommended loss for most contrastive fine-tuning scenarios because it requires only (query, positive) pairs, has no sensitive hyperparameters, handles in-batch negatives automatically, and converges reliably. The sentence-transformers library implements it as a first-class loss, making integration straightforward.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses, evaluation
from sentence_transformers.evaluation import InformationRetrievalEvaluator
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader


# ── 1. Load a pre-trained base model ─────────────────────────────────────────
model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")

# ── 2. Prepare (query, positive_passage) training pairs ──────────────────────
# You need at minimum 1,000 pairs for meaningful fine-tuning.
# 10,000–100,000 pairs is typical for production models.
train_examples = [
    InputExample(texts=[
        "What is the mechanism of aspirin?",
        "Aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2 by acetylation "
        "of a serine residue, reducing prostaglandin and thromboxane synthesis.",
    ]),
    InputExample(texts=[
        "Side effects of metformin in type 2 diabetes",
        "The most common metformin side effects are gastrointestinal: nausea, diarrhoea, "
        "and abdominal discomfort. Lactic acidosis is rare but serious, especially in renal impairment.",
    ]),
    InputExample(texts=[
        "How is haemoglobin A1c used to monitor diabetes?",
        "HbA1c reflects average blood glucose over the preceding 2–3 months by measuring "
        "the percentage of haemoglobin that is glycated. A target below 7% is standard for "
        "most type 2 diabetes patients.",
    ]),
    InputExample(texts=[
        "What is the half-life of amoxicillin?",
        "Amoxicillin has a half-life of approximately 1–1.5 hours in adults with normal renal function. "
        "It is typically dosed every 8 hours to maintain adequate plasma concentrations.",
    ]),
    # ... add thousands more pairs ...
]

# ── 3. DataLoader with appropriate batch size ─────────────────────────────────
# Large batches → more in-batch negatives → better quality
# Typical: 32–256 depending on GPU memory
train_loader = DataLoader(train_examples, shuffle=True, batch_size=32)

# ── 4. Loss function ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
loss_fn = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model=model)

# ── 5. Evaluation on a held-out retrieval benchmark ──────────────────────────
# InformationRetrievalEvaluator computes NDCG@10, MRR@10, Recall@k
eval_queries  = {"q1": "What is aspirin used for?"}
eval_corpus   = {"d1": "Aspirin is used for pain, fever, and inflammation.",
                 "d2": "Python is a programming language.",
                 "d3": "Amoxicillin treats bacterial infections."}
eval_relevant = {"q1": {"d1"}}
evaluator = InformationRetrievalEvaluator(
    eval_queries, eval_corpus, eval_relevant,
    name="biomedical-dev",
    ndcg_at_k=[10],
    recall_at_k=[5, 10],
)

# ── 6. Train ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
model.fit(
    train_objectives=[(train_loader, loss_fn)],
    epochs=3,
    warmup_steps=max(1, int(0.1 * len(train_loader) * 3)),
    evaluator=evaluator,
    evaluation_steps=max(1, len(train_loader) // 2),
    output_path="./biomedical-bge-fine-tuned",
    save_best_model=True,
    show_progress_bar=True,
    # optimizer_params={"lr": 2e-5},
)

# ── 7. Inference ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fine_tuned = SentenceTransformer("./biomedical-bge-fine-tuned")
q_vec = fine_tuned.encode(["mechanism of aspirin"], normalize_embeddings=True)
d_vecs = fine_tuned.encode([
    "Aspirin inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes.",
    "Python is a scripting language.",
    "Warfarin antagonises vitamin K.",
], normalize_embeddings=True)
scores = (q_vec @ d_vecs.T)[0]
for doc, score in sorted(zip(["aspirin", "python", "warfarin"], scores), key=lambda x: -x[1]):
    print(f"  {score:.4f}  {doc}")
# 0.9321  aspirin
# 0.5412  warfarin
# 0.2134  python
Positive and Negative Pair Construction

attrs.alt: Negative sampling strategies: in-batch negatives, hard negatives, and SimCSE self-supervised approach

content.text: What Makes a Good Positive

A positive pair consists of two texts that a user querying one would consider the other a valid answer for. Good positives capture genuine semantic equivalence at the level of the task, not just surface-level keyword overlap. For retrieval tasks:
Query–passage pairs where the passage directly answers the query (from QA datasets, FAQ pairs).
Title–body pairs from Wikipedia, news articles, or documentation.
Translated sentence pairs (if the index is multilingual).
Paraphrase pairs (semantic textual similarity datasets like MRPC, QQP).
Duplicate forum post pairs (StackOverflow, Quora Question Pairs).
Document–sub-section pairs (a document is semantically related to its own sections).

Poor positives — for example, pairs that share keywords but have different meanings, or pairs that are related but only tangentially — produce poor training signal. The model learns to associate surface form rather than meaning.
In-Batch Negatives

The simplest strategy is to treat every other passage in the batch as a negative for each anchor. With batch size B, each anchor gets B-1 negatives 'for free' with no additional labelling cost. The quality of in-batch negatives depends entirely on batch composition: if all passages in the batch are from the same narrow domain, many will be near-positives and produce false-negative training signal.

A practical mitigation is to shuffle the dataset thoroughly and use a large batch size. With B=256, each query sees 255 negatives, statistically covering a diverse range of topics. With B=16, the negatives are often too few and too easy.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
import random

# Correct way: shuffle pairs BEFORE creating DataLoader
# to ensure in-batch negatives are diverse
pairs = [
    ("query about topic A", "answer about topic A"),
    ("query about topic B", "answer about topic B"),
    # Imagine 10,000 diverse pairs spanning many topics
]

random.shuffle(pairs)

# Large batch ensures diverse in-batch negatives
train_examples = [InputExample(texts=[q, p]) for q, p in pairs]
train_loader = DataLoader(train_examples, shuffle=True, batch_size=128)

model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
loss_fn = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model=model)

# Monitor for false negatives: add deduplication pass
# to ensure no query's positive appears as another query's negative in same batch
def check_false_negatives(batch_pairs):
    """Detect if any positive passage appears more than once in the same batch."""
    passages = [p for _, p in batch_pairs]
    seen = set()
    false_neg_count = 0
    for p in passages:
        if p in seen:
            false_neg_count += 1
        seen.add(p)
    return false_neg_count

false_neg_rate = check_false_negatives(pairs[:128])
print(f"False negatives in first batch: {false_neg_rate} / {len(pairs[:128])}")
Hard Negative Mining

Hard negatives are passages that are semantically close to the anchor but are NOT its true positive. They are the most informative training signal because they force the model to learn fine-grained distinctions. In biomedical retrieval, 'Ibuprofen inhibits COX enzymes' is a hard negative for 'Aspirin inhibits COX enzymes' — they look similar but are about different drugs.

Hard negatives are mined by encoding the full corpus, building an ANN index, and retrieving the top-k nearest neighbours for each query. Neighbours that are not the true positive are hard negatives. The margin parameter filters out passages that are too close to the positive — passages within the margin of the true positive may actually be relevant and treating them as negatives introduces label noise.

import numpy as np
import faiss
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer


def mine_hard_negatives(
    queries: list[str],
    corpus: list[str],
    positive_indices: list[int],   # corpus index of the positive for each query
    model: SentenceTransformer,
    margin: float = 0.1,           # neg must score <= pos_score - margin
    n_candidates: int = 100,       # ANN candidates retrieved per query
    n_negatives: int = 5,          # hard negatives to keep per query
) -> list[tuple[str, str, str]]:
    """
    Mine hard negatives from a corpus for each (query, positive) pair.
    Returns (anchor, positive, hard_negative) triplets.
    """
    print("Encoding corpus...")
    corp_vecs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True,
                              batch_size=256, show_progress_bar=True)
    q_vecs    = model.encode(queries, normalize_embeddings=True, batch_size=256)

    # Build FAISS flat index for exact cosine retrieval
    dim = corp_vecs.shape[1]
    index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)
    index.add(corp_vecs.astype(np.float32))
    print(f"Index built: {index.ntotal} vectors, dim={dim}")

    scores_all, indices_all = index.search(q_vecs.astype(np.float32), n_candidates)

    triplets = []
    skipped_false_neg = 0
    for qi, (q_scores, q_indices) in enumerate(zip(scores_all, indices_all)):
        pos_idx   = positive_indices[qi]
        pos_score = float(corp_vecs[pos_idx] @ q_vecs[qi])

        negs_found = []
        for cand_idx, cand_score in zip(q_indices, q_scores):
            if cand_idx == pos_idx:
                continue  # Skip true positive
            if cand_score > pos_score - margin:
                # Too close to positive — potential false negative, skip
                skipped_false_neg += 1
                continue
            negs_found.append(corpus[cand_idx])
            if len(negs_found) >= n_negatives:
                break

        for neg in negs_found:
            triplets.append((queries[qi], corpus[pos_idx], neg))

    print(f"Mined {len(triplets)} hard-negative triplets; "
          f"skipped {skipped_false_neg} potential false negatives.")
    return triplets


# Example usage
model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")

queries  = ["What is aspirin's mechanism?", "How does caffeine work?"]
corpus   = [
    "Aspirin inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 irreversibly.",      # pos for q0
    "Aspirin is used for pain, fever, and inflammation.",   # plausible (skip — close)
    "Ibuprofen also inhibits COX but reversibly.",          # hard neg for q0
    "NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin synthesis.",               # hard neg for q0
    "Caffeine blocks adenosine receptors.",                 # pos for q1
    "Caffeine has a half-life of 5-6 hours.",               # hard neg for q1
    "Theophylline is another methylxanthine like caffeine.", # hard neg for q1
    "Python is a programming language.",                   # easy neg
]
positive_indices = [0, 4]

triplets = mine_hard_negatives(queries, corpus, positive_indices, model,
                               margin=0.1, n_candidates=8, n_negatives=2)
for a, p, n in triplets:
    print(f"  A: {a}")
    print(f"  P: {p[:60]}")
    print(f"  N: {n[:60]}")
    print()
Self-Supervised Contrastive Learning: SimCSE

SimCSE (Simple Contrastive Learning of Sentence Embeddings, Gao et al. 2021) is a self-supervised variant that requires no labelled pairs at all. The key insight is that dropout in transformer layers provides a natural augmentation: passing the same sentence through the encoder twice with different dropout masks produces two slightly different embeddings that the model should treat as a positive pair.

Unsupervised SimCSE uses any unlabelled text corpus. Supervised SimCSE builds on natural language inference (NLI) datasets: entailment pairs (sentence + entailment hypothesis) are positives, and contradiction pairs are hard negatives. Supervised SimCSE consistently outperforms the unsupervised variant by 5–10 points on STS benchmarks.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

# ── Unsupervised SimCSE ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# No labels required. Each sentence is its own positive.
# Different dropout masks produce subtly different embeddings.

model = SentenceTransformer("bert-base-uncased")

# Domain corpus: collect from your target domain (clinical notes, code, legal docs)
domain_sentences = [
    "Aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes.",
    "Warfarin requires INR monitoring to prevent bleeding complications.",
    "Type 2 diabetes is characterised by insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency.",
    "Metformin is first-line pharmacotherapy for type 2 diabetes.",
    "Amoxicillin is a broad-spectrum penicillin antibiotic.",
    # Use 10,000–500,000 sentences from your domain corpus
]

# Duplicate: each sentence is paired with itself
unsup_examples = [InputExample(texts=[s, s]) for s in domain_sentences]
unsup_loader   = DataLoader(unsup_examples, batch_size=64, shuffle=True)
loss_fn_unsup  = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model=model)

model.fit(
    train_objectives=[(unsup_loader, loss_fn_unsup)],
    epochs=1,
    warmup_steps=len(unsup_loader) // 10,
    output_path="./simcse-unsupervised-biomedical",
    show_progress_bar=True,
)

# ── Supervised SimCSE (NLI-based) ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Requires labelled NLI data: (premise, entailment, contradiction) triples.
# The third element in InputExample.texts is treated as a hard negative by
# MultipleNegativesRankingLoss when cache_labels=True.

model_sup = SentenceTransformer("bert-base-uncased")

nli_examples = [
    InputExample(texts=[
        "A patient presents with acute chest pain and elevated troponin.",   # premise
        "The patient may be experiencing a myocardial infarction.",          # entailment (positive)
        "The patient's cardiac enzymes are entirely normal.",               # contradiction (hard neg)
    ]),
    InputExample(texts=[
        "The drug has a narrow therapeutic index.",
        "The drug requires careful dosage monitoring.",
        "The drug can be taken in any amount without adverse effects.",
    ]),
    InputExample(texts=[
        "Penicillin is effective against gram-positive bacteria.",
        "Penicillin can treat streptococcal infections.",
        "Penicillin has no antibacterial activity.",
    ]),
]

sup_loader  = DataLoader(nli_examples, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
loss_fn_sup = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model=model_sup)

model_sup.fit(
    train_objectives=[(sup_loader, loss_fn_sup)],
    epochs=3,
    warmup_steps=len(sup_loader) // 5,
    output_path="./simcse-supervised-biomedical",
    show_progress_bar=True,
)

# Evaluate: compare isotropy (uniformity) before and after
def uniformity_loss(embeddings):
    """Wang & Isola uniformity metric: how spread out are embeddings on the hypersphere?
    Lower (more negative) = more uniform = better. Ideal: ~-2 to -3 for well-trained model."""
    import torch
    emb = torch.tensor(embeddings)
    pdist = torch.pdist(emb, p=2).pow(2)
    return torch.log(torch.exp(-2 * pdist).mean()).item()

unsup_model = SentenceTransformer("./simcse-unsupervised-biomedical")
vecs = unsup_model.encode(domain_sentences, normalize_embeddings=True)
print(f"Uniformity after SimCSE unsupervised: {uniformity_loss(vecs):.3f}")

The uniformity metric (Wang & Isola, 2020) measures how evenly embeddings are spread on the unit hypersphere. Pre-training on masked LM produces highly anisotropic (clustered) embeddings with uniformity near 0. After SimCSE training, uniformity drops to around -2 to -3, indicating the embeddings are more spread out and better calibrated for cosine similarity.
When to Use Contrastive Learning
You have a retrieval task (semantic search, RAG) and want a model tailored to your domain vocabulary and query patterns.
You have access to (query, relevant_document) pairs from user logs, curated FAQ, or QA datasets — even 1,000–5,000 pairs can meaningfully improve a general model.
Your domain uses specialised terminology not well-represented in general pre-training data (biomedical, legal, scientific, code).
You need zero-cost domain adaptation and have a large corpus of domain text (unsupervised SimCSE).
You want to compress a large embedding model into a smaller one via knowledge distillation, using the large model's similarity scores as soft labels for contrastive training of the small model.
You are building a cross-lingual retrieval system and want to align embedding spaces across languages using translation-pair supervision.

Contrastive fine-tuning is NOT needed if a strong general model already works well on your domain (measure recall@10 on a sample first — if it exceeds 0.85, the ROI on fine-tuning is low). It is also not needed for tasks where the encoder is frozen and only a downstream classifier is trained.
How to Implement: Full Training Pipeline

The full contrastive fine-tuning pipeline has six stages: data collection, data formatting, base model selection, loss function configuration, training loop, and evaluation. The sentence-transformers library handles the vast majority of this with minimal boilerplate.

"""
Full contrastive fine-tuning pipeline with hard negatives.
Assumes you have a CSV of (query, positive_passage) pairs.
"""
import csv
import json
from pathlib import Path
from sentence_transformers import (
    SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses,
    evaluation
)
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

# ── Step 1: Load data ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def load_pairs(csv_path: str) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
    """Load (query, passage) CSV. Expected columns: query, passage."""
    pairs = []
    with open(csv_path, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        for row in csv.DictReader(f):
            q = row["query"].strip()
            p = row["passage"].strip()
            if q and p:
                pairs.append((q, p))
    return pairs

# ── Step 2: Train/dev split ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
import random
all_pairs = load_pairs("domain_pairs.csv") if Path("domain_pairs.csv").exists() else [
    ("What is aspirin?",       "Aspirin inhibits COX-1 and COX-2."),
    ("How does insulin work?", "Insulin binds its receptor and triggers GLUT4 translocation."),
    ("What is HbA1c?",         "HbA1c measures average blood glucose over 2-3 months."),
]
random.shuffle(all_pairs)
split = int(0.9 * len(all_pairs))
train_pairs = all_pairs[:split]
dev_pairs   = all_pairs[split:]

# ── Step 3: Build training examples ──────────────────────────────────────────
train_examples = [InputExample(texts=[q, p]) for q, p in train_pairs]
train_loader   = DataLoader(train_examples, shuffle=True, batch_size=32)

# ── Step 4: Base model — choose one with strong MTEB retrieval performance ────
base_model = "BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5"    # 768-dim, strong general retrieval
# Alternative: "BAAI/bge-m3"            # multilingual
# Alternative: "nomic-ai/nomic-embed-text-v1.5"  # 8192 token context
model = SentenceTransformer(base_model)

# ── Step 5: Loss ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
loss_fn = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model=model)

# ── Step 6: Evaluator from dev set ───────────────────────────────────────────
dev_queries   = {str(i): q for i, (q, _) in enumerate(dev_pairs)}
dev_corpus    = {str(i): p for i, (_, p) in enumerate(dev_pairs)}
dev_relevant  = {str(i): {str(i)} for i in range(len(dev_pairs))}
evaluator = evaluation.InformationRetrievalEvaluator(
    dev_queries, dev_corpus, dev_relevant,
    name="dev",
    ndcg_at_k=[10],
    recall_at_k=[5, 10],
)

# ── Step 7: Train ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
n_epochs     = 3
warmup_steps = max(1, int(0.06 * len(train_loader) * n_epochs))
model.fit(
    train_objectives=[(train_loader, loss_fn)],
    epochs=n_epochs,
    warmup_steps=warmup_steps,
    evaluator=evaluator,
    evaluation_steps=max(1, len(train_loader)),   # evaluate after each epoch
    output_path="./domain-contrastive-model",
    save_best_model=True,
    show_progress_bar=True,
)
print("Training complete. Best model saved to ./domain-contrastive-model")
Examples
Simple Example: STS Pair Training

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
import numpy as np

# Minimal contrastive training: symmetric semantic similarity pairs
model = SentenceTransformer("paraphrase-MiniLM-L6-v2")

# Positive pairs: semantically equivalent sentences
train_examples = [
    InputExample(texts=["How do I install Python?",  "Steps to set up Python."]),
    InputExample(texts=["What is gradient descent?", "Explain the gradient descent algorithm."]),
    InputExample(texts=["How to sort a list?",       "Python list sorting methods."]),
]

loader  = DataLoader(train_examples, batch_size=3, shuffle=True)
loss_fn = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model=model)

model.fit(train_objectives=[(loader, loss_fn)], epochs=2, show_progress_bar=False)

# Test: similar queries should now score higher
test_texts = [
    "How to install Python on Windows?",
    "Python installation guide.",
    "What is Java?",
    "Java programming tutorial.",
]
vecs = model.encode(test_texts, normalize_embeddings=True)
print("Similarity matrix after training:")
sim = vecs @ vecs.T
for i, t1 in enumerate(test_texts):
    for j, t2 in enumerate(test_texts):
        if j > i:
            print(f"  {sim[i,j]:.3f}  '{t1[:30]}' vs '{t2[:30]}'")
# Expected: Python pairs score ~0.9, Java pair scores ~0.8, cross-topic pairs ~0.3
Advanced Example: Evaluate Recall@k Before and After Fine-Tuning

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np


def recall_at_k(
    model: SentenceTransformer,
    queries: list[str],
    corpus: list[str],
    relevant_ids: list[list[int]],
    k: int = 10,
) -> float:
    q_vecs = model.encode(queries, normalize_embeddings=True, batch_size=128)
    c_vecs = model.encode(corpus,  normalize_embeddings=True, batch_size=128)
    sim = q_vecs @ c_vecs.T                  # (Q, C)
    ranked = np.argsort(-sim, axis=1)[:, :k] # top-k indices per query
    recalls = []
    for i, rel in enumerate(relevant_ids):
        if rel:
            hits = len(set(ranked[i].tolist()) & set(rel))
            recalls.append(hits / len(rel))
    return float(np.mean(recalls)) if recalls else 0.0


# Benchmark corpus and queries
queries = [
    "mechanism of aspirin",
    "insulin resistance measurement",
    "half life of amoxicillin",
]
corpus  = [
    "Aspirin inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 by irreversible acetylation.",    # rel to q0
    "Aspirin reduces fever, pain, and inflammation.",                    # rel to q0
    "HOMA-IR = (fasting glucose x fasting insulin) / 22.5.",            # rel to q1
    "Values above 2.0 on HOMA-IR suggest insulin resistance.",          # rel to q1
    "Amoxicillin has a half-life of approximately 1-1.5 hours.",        # rel to q2
    "Ibuprofen is an NSAID similar to aspirin.",                        # irrelevant
    "Python is a programming language.",                                # irrelevant
]
relevant = [[0, 1], [2, 3], [4]]

# Base model performance
base = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
r_base = recall_at_k(base, queries, corpus, relevant, k=5)
print(f"Base recall@5:       {r_base:.3f}")

# After fine-tuning (assuming model was saved)
# fine_tuned = SentenceTransformer("./domain-contrastive-model")
# r_ft = recall_at_k(fine_tuned, queries, corpus, relevant, k=5)
# print(f"Fine-tuned recall@5: {r_ft:.3f}")
# Typical improvement: 0.70 base → 0.91 fine-tuned on domain-specific benchmark
Real-World Example: Biomedical Literature Retrieval

A common production use case is fine-tuning an embedding model for PubMed-style retrieval. Clinical question-answering systems need to retrieve relevant abstracts for queries like 'what are the contraindications for warfarin in elderly patients with renal impairment?' — queries that use clinical intent patterns very different from general web search.

The training data is typically sourced from PubMed QA datasets (PubMedQA, BioASQ), clinical guidelines (NICE, UpToDate), and internal EHR query logs. The evaluation benchmark is a held-out set of (clinical question, relevant abstract) pairs with annotated relevance.

"""
Biomedical retrieval fine-tuning with hard negatives.
Simulates a production scenario with clinical query-abstract pairs.
"""
import numpy as np
import faiss
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader


# ── Simulate PubMed-like corpus and QA pairs ─────────────────────────────────
clinical_qa_pairs = [
    ("Mechanism of warfarin anticoagulation",
     "Warfarin inhibits VKORC1, preventing recycling of vitamin K, which is required "
     "for activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. The anticoagulant effect "
     "is measured by the INR."),
    ("Contraindications of warfarin",
     "Warfarin is contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic, especially in weeks 6-12), "
     "active major bleeding, and severe hepatic impairment. Relative contraindications "
     "include falls risk and poor medication adherence."),
    ("Warfarin drug interactions",
     "Warfarin has numerous interactions: CYP2C9 inhibitors (fluconazole, amiodarone) "
     "increase warfarin levels; enzyme inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine) decrease them. "
     "NSAIDs increase bleeding risk by inhibiting platelet aggregation."),
    ("Direct oral anticoagulants vs warfarin",
     "DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran) have predictable pharmacokinetics, "
     "require no routine monitoring, and have fewer food/drug interactions. They have "
     "largely replaced warfarin in non-valvular atrial fibrillation."),
]

# ── Step 1: Expand with hard negatives ───────────────────────────────────────
# Mine hard negatives using a seed model
seed_model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
corpus_texts    = [p for _, p in clinical_qa_pairs]
corpus_vecs     = seed_model.encode(corpus_texts, normalize_embeddings=True)
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(corpus_vecs.shape[1])
index.add(corpus_vecs.astype(np.float32))

extended_examples = []
for qi, (query, positive) in enumerate(clinical_qa_pairs):
    q_vec = seed_model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)
    pos_score = float(corpus_vecs[qi] @ q_vec[0])
    scores, indices = index.search(q_vec.astype(np.float32), 4)

    hard_negs = []
    for cand_idx, cand_score in zip(indices[0], scores[0]):
        if cand_idx == qi:
            continue  # Skip self
        if cand_score < pos_score - 0.05:  # Must be meaningfully worse
            hard_negs.append(corpus_texts[cand_idx])
        if len(hard_negs) >= 1:
            break

    for neg in hard_negs:
        extended_examples.append(InputExample(texts=[query, positive, neg]))
    extended_examples.append(InputExample(texts=[query, positive]))

print(f"Training examples: {len(extended_examples)} "
      f"({sum(1 for e in extended_examples if len(e.texts)==3)} with hard negs)")

# ── Step 2: Train ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
model = SentenceTransformer("ncats/sapbert-from-pubmedbert-fulltext")  # biomedical base
loader  = DataLoader(extended_examples, shuffle=True, batch_size=8)
loss_fn = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model=model)
model.fit(
    train_objectives=[(loader, loss_fn)],
    epochs=5,
    output_path="./clinical-contrastive-model",
    show_progress_bar=True,
)

# ── Step 3: Index and query ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
fine_tuned = SentenceTransformer("./clinical-contrastive-model")
doc_vecs   = fine_tuned.encode(corpus_texts, normalize_embeddings=True)
faiss_idx  = faiss.IndexFlatIP(doc_vecs.shape[1])
faiss_idx.add(doc_vecs.astype(np.float32))

query = "Is warfarin safe in pregnancy?"
q_vec = fine_tuned.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)
scores, indices = faiss_idx.search(q_vec.astype(np.float32), 3)
print(f"Query: '{query}'")
for rank, (idx, score) in enumerate(zip(indices[0], scores[0]), 1):
    print(f"  {rank}. ({score:.3f}) {corpus_texts[idx][:80]}...")
Common Mistakes
Not normalising embeddings before computing similarity: raw dot product is scale-dependent; L2-normalise before any cosine-based loss or FAISS IndexFlatIP usage.
Using a batch size that is too small: with batch size 8–16, in-batch negatives are too few and too easy, and the model converges to a weak local minimum. Minimum: 32; recommended: 64–256.

Ignoring false negatives: in a corpus where many passages are relevant to a given query, treating all non-positive passages in the batch as negatives corrupts the gradient. At minimum, deduplicate the positive set and check that no positive appears as a negative for another query in the same batch.
Choosing a temperature that is too low: tau < 0.03 produces explosive gradients when any negative has similarity close to 1.0, causing NaN losses. Start at 0.07 and tune from there.
Fine-tuning all layers on a tiny dataset: fine-tuning all 110M parameters of a BERT model on 500 pairs catastrophically overfits. Freeze the early layers or use LoRA.

Not evaluating on retrieval metrics: evaluating on STS cosine similarity (Spearman rho) is not a reliable proxy for retrieval recall@k. Always measure recall@5 and recall@10 on a held-out retrieval benchmark.

Mixing symmetric and asymmetric usage: if your use case is asymmetric (short query vs. long document), use a model that supports query/passage prefixes (E5, BGE) and apply them consistently. Using a symmetric model for asymmetric retrieval systematically underperforms.
Not using gradient clipping: contrastive losses can produce large gradient spikes with hard negatives. Clip at norm 1.0 to stabilise training.
Best Practices

Start from a strong general model: fine-tuning BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5 with 5,000 domain pairs produces better results than training a domain model from scratch. The general model already captures linguistic structure.
Use MultipleNegativesRankingLoss as your default loss: it is simpler, more stable, and empirically competitive with more complex losses.

Implement a two-stage curriculum: train first with in-batch negatives for 1–2 epochs to build a reasonable embedding space, then mine hard negatives from the updated index and train for another 1–3 epochs.
Deduplicate your training corpus before building the index for hard negative mining: near-duplicate passages produce noisy false negatives.
Use warmup steps (5–10% of training steps): the early gradient updates from contrastive loss can be very large; warmup prevents early divergence.

Evaluate alignment and uniformity in addition to recall@k: alignment (average similarity of positive pairs) should increase; uniformity (spread of embeddings on the sphere) should decrease from its masked-LM baseline.
Maintain a fixed random seed for reproducibility: the batch composition affects in-batch negatives, which changes the gradient. Set torch.manual_seed and DataLoader worker seeds.
Version your training data and model checkpoints: contrastive models are highly sensitive to the training distribution; if the data changes, you will need to re-mine hard negatives.
Performance and Scalability

Contrastive training is more GPU-memory intensive than classification because each batch must encode 2B texts (anchor + positive), compute the full (B, B) similarity matrix, and backpropagate through the complete encoder for each. With bert-base and batch size 64, a single step uses about 8 GB of GPU memory.
Gradient checkpointing (model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()) reduces memory by ~40% at the cost of ~20% slower training — essential for batch sizes > 64 on smaller GPUs.
Mixed precision (fp16/bf16) halves memory and doubles throughput on modern GPUs with minimal quality impact. Use torch.autocast('cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16).

Distributed training with multiple GPUs: cross-device negative sharing (GatherLayer in SimCLR) allows the effective batch size to be N_GPUs × batch_size_per_gpu, dramatically increasing the number of in-batch negatives.

ANN index updates for hard negative mining: rebuilding the full FAISS index after each epoch is expensive for large corpora (>1M documents). A practical compromise is to update the index every 2–5 epochs or use an approximate iterative mining scheme.
Inference throughput: after training, the encoder can be exported to ONNX and served with ONNX Runtime, achieving 2–5x faster inference than PyTorch for batch sizes 1–16.

Hard negative mining with FAISS scales approximately linearly with corpus size for HNSW indexes. Encoding a 1M-document corpus with BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5 takes about 20–30 minutes on a single A100 GPU. The ANN search itself takes under 1 minute for 1M documents.

# Using the Beir benchmark to evaluate a fine-tuned contrastive model
from beir import util, LoggingHandler
from beir.datasets.data_loader import GenericDataLoader
from beir.retrieval.evaluation import EvaluateRetrieval
from beir.retrieval import models
from beir.retrieval.search.dense import DenseRetrievalExactSearch as DRES

# Load a Beir dataset (e.g. TREC-COVID biomedical retrieval)
url = "https://public.ukp.informatik.tu-darmstadt.de/thakur/BEIR/datasets/trec-covid.zip"
# data_path = util.download_and_unzip(url, "datasets")  # uncomment to download

# Wrap our contrastive model in Beir's SentenceBERT wrapper
model_beir = DRES(
    models.SentenceBERT("./domain-contrastive-model"),
    batch_size=256,
    corpus_chunk_size=512,
)
retriever = EvaluateRetrieval(model_beir, score_function="cos_sim", k_values=[1, 5, 10, 50])

# Evaluate (assuming data_path is set)
# corpus, queries, qrels = GenericDataLoader(data_folder=data_path).load(split="test")
# results = retriever.retrieve(corpus, queries)
# ndcg, map_, recall, precision = retriever.evaluate(qrels, results, retriever.k_values)
# print(f"NDCG@10: {ndcg['NDCG@10']:.4f}")  # Compare base vs fine-tuned

Contrastive fine-tuning sits between a general embedding model and a cross-encoder in the quality-cost tradeoff. For production systems serving more than a few hundred queries per second, contrastive fine-tuning is almost always the right choice over full cross-encoder reranking of the entire corpus.
Security and Reliability

Contrastive embedding models can be attacked via embedding inversion: given a model's output vector, an attacker can approximately reconstruct the input text using gradient-based inversion. For sensitive documents (medical records, legal documents), the embedding itself is sensitive data and should be treated as such — stored encrypted, not returned to clients, and served only through access-controlled APIs.
Do not expose raw embedding vectors to untrusted clients in retrieval APIs. Return only document content or document IDs, not their embedding vectors.

Guard against training data extraction: contrastive models can memorise rare training examples. If training data is sensitive (private medical texts), prefer federated learning or differential privacy training.
Monitor for embedding drift: if the document corpus changes substantially, the trained model's retrieval quality degrades. Implement periodic re-evaluation of recall@k on a canary evaluation set.
Validate inputs at inference time: very long inputs (>4096 tokens) can cause silent truncation. Implement a length check and return a warning or chunk the input before encoding.

Adversarial queries: embedding-based retrieval can be fooled by queries designed to maximally exploit the model's geometry. For high-stakes applications (medical triage, legal discovery), supplement retrieval with keyword filters and human review.
Mental Model

Think of contrastive learning as training a magnet embedded inside the encoder. During training, semantically similar texts develop a strong magnetic attraction to each other in the embedding space, while dissimilar texts develop repulsion. The encoder is the 'magnet field generator' — its weights determine the geometry of attraction and repulsion.

Before training, the magnetic field is random (the masked-LM embedding space). The contrastive objective applies a systematic force to this field: every gradient update nudges the field slightly so that the observed positive pairs attract more strongly, and the seen negatives repel more strongly. After thousands of updates over millions of pairs, the field has a coherent structure where semantic categories cluster together naturally.

The temperature parameter in InfoNCE is the inverse strength of the magnetic signal: low temperature means a very strong, precise signal that strongly differentiates hard negatives; high temperature is a gentle, diffuse signal. Too low, and the signal is noisy and unstable (the equivalent of trying to magnetise steel with a spark); too high, and the signal is too weak to rearrange the field significantly.

Hard negatives are 'near-misses' in this metaphor — texts that end up close in the magnetic field despite being semantically different. Mining hard negatives and including them in training is equivalent to specifically targeting the parts of the field that are incorrectly magnetised and correcting them.
Key Takeaways

Contrastive learning reshapes the embedding space so that semantically similar texts cluster together and dissimilar ones are separated — this is the mechanism behind every modern high-quality embedding model.

MultipleNegativesRankingLoss is the recommended loss for most fine-tuning scenarios: it requires only (query, positive) pairs, uses all other passages in the batch as negatives, and converges reliably without temperature tuning.
Large batch size is critical for in-batch negative quality: aim for at least 64–128; each additional pair in the batch adds a free negative for every other query.

Hard negative mining — identifying near-miss passages using ANN search — dramatically improves model quality beyond what in-batch negatives alone can achieve. Implement a two-stage curriculum: soft negatives first, hard negatives after.
False negatives (passages that ARE relevant but are treated as negatives) corrupt the gradient. Deduplicate the training corpus and use a margin filter when mining hard negatives.
SimCSE provides a zero-annotation path to domain adaptation: pass each domain sentence through the encoder twice with different dropout masks and treat the two outputs as a positive pair.
Always evaluate on retrieval metrics (recall@k, NDCG@10) rather than STS similarity — the two do not perfectly correlate and retrieval is the actual use case.
After fine-tuning, export to ONNX for 2–5x inference speedup. The embedding geometry is fixed after training; no fine-tuning changes are needed at inference time.
